content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

March 13, 1991

Jesus Armas
City Manager
City of South San Francisco
400 Grand Avenue
P.O. Box 711
South San Francisco, CA  94083

Re:  Your Request for Advice
     Our File No. A-90-499a

Dear Mr. Armas:

     I am writing in response to your letter seeking confirmation
of our December 13, 1990 telephone conversation pertaining to
Councilmember Penna and the law regarding "participation" under
the Political Reform Act.

     You stated that you presented written information to the city
council regarding a potential property transaction.  You provided
this information to the councilmembers to enable them to discuss
potential disposition of the property.  However, you did not
provide this information to Mr. Penna because we had advised that
he may not participate in certain decisions regarding the
Shearwater redevelopment project. (Armento Advice Letter, No. A-
90-499.)

     Subsequently, Mr. Penna requested copies of the written
information and you inquired whether Mr. Penna may be provided
this information.  I advised you that there are neither provisions
in the Political Reform Act nor any regulations adopted pursuant
to the Act which would preclude your furnishing Mr. Penna with the
same information you provided the other councilmembers.  An
official may be informed, even if he cannot participate in the
decision.

     Under Government Code Section 87100, an official who knows or
has reason to know of a financial interest in a decision may not
make, participate in making, or use his official position to

428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660

Our File No. A-90-499a
Page 2


influence that decision. Regulation 18700 and 18700.1¹ (copies
enclosed), which we discussed, implement this section. Regulation
18700 defines "participating in a governmental decision" as
negotiating, advising, recommending, researching, investigating,
and using independent judgment to reach a decision. Regulation
18700.1 defines when an official is using his or her official
position to influence a decision. As can be seen, the regulations
only address participating or influencing a governmental decision.

You also sent me a copy of Hamilton v. Town of Los Gatos
(1989) 213 Cal. App. 3d 1050, 261 Cal. Rptr. 888. This court held
that a disqualified councilmember may not attend a closed session
meeting, nor obtain access to the tape recording of that meeting.
However, the appellate court did not address the issue of open
meetings and presenting a disqualified member with information.
Since we have never interpreted presence as participation, we do
not apply Hamilton to preclude the presence of a disqualified
member at an open meeting.

If you presented information in written form to the city
council to enable them to discuss potential disposition of city
property and if such discussion is scheduled for a closed meeting,
Hamilton could be read to preclude providing the information to
the disqualified councilmember. As stated by the court in
Hamilton, supra, 213 Cal. App. 3d at 1058:

To permit a financially interested council
member to be privy, unnecessarily, to confidential
information which might affect his business
interests gives the appearance of impropriety. In
our society, information is power. The council
member might use the confidential information to
his advantage personally, or her might disclose the
information improperly to others interested in the
decision. Furthermore, the disqualified member's
mere presence, or knowledge thereafter, might also
subtly influence the decisions of other council
members who must maintain an ongoing relationship
with him. (Footnote omitted.)

If however, you presented information to the city council,
and such information is scheduled for discussion at an open
meeting, it does not appear that Hamilton would preclude
Councilmember Penna from receiving this information.


¹ Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

Our File No. A-90-499a
Page 3


I trust this letter answers your question. You may also want
to contact the Attorney General's office for advice under the
Brown Act, which is not under our jurisdiction.

Sincerely,

Scott Hallabrin
Acting General Counsel

By: Jill Stecher
Counsel, Legal Division

SH:JS:plh

Enclosures
